perturbation.original_text: Base Product means the DSP Technology including the ON Semiconductor Ezairo or SCHA chips, the EEPROM and the firmware loaded onto the EEPROM and the minimum printed circuit board package necessary to connect and house these items. The Base Product does not include additional PCB or flex circuits necessary to connect to other components of an Enhanced Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Base Product, also referred to as 'Core Component,' means the DSP Technology including the ON Semiconductor Ezairo or SCHA chips, the EEPROM and the firmware loaded onto the EEPROM and the minimum printed circuit board package necessary to connect and house these items. The Base Product does not include additional PCB or flex circuits necessary to connect to other components of an Enhanced Product.
perturbation.explanation: The introduction of 'Core Component' as an interchangeable term for 'Base Product' in section 2.9 creates confusion. Although later references consistently use 'Base Product,' the initial interchangeable definition introduces uncertainty, particularly in financial clauses or those related to product liability. This change makes it unclear if 'Core Component' is a subset or identical to the 'Base Product' specifically in sections regarding payments and minimum royalties. The definition of 'Base Product' is critically tied to payments. Creating ambiguity undermines the financial section.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Royalty Payments will be made only on the Base Product Unit, or on the Base Product portion of an Enhanced Product for any Base Product included in an Enhanced Product. The initial base rate of royalty shall include the use of the DSP Framework. In no case shall IntriCon pay Royalty Payments on any product or portion of any product other than for revenue received for Base Product Unit or for the Base Product portion of an Enhanced Product, and in the case a Base Product portion of an Enhanced Product such Base Product portion shall not be given a value that is influenced by its inclusion in the Enhanced Product. Royalty Payment for HH, ALD and PADA units that incorporate Dynamic Hearing Technology shall be paid according to the table set forth below: The maximum royalty rate identified in the table herein includes an initial base rate of 3% for each Ezairo DSP platform and 1% for each Single Chip Hearing Aid (SCHA) sold. A 2% royalty rate per Base Product Unit shall be added to the initial base rate for each DSP feature/module that is based on Dynamic Hearing Technology and that is added to a Base Product Unit. However, such additional Royalty Payments when added to the initial base rate shall not exceed in total the stated maximum rates specified in the table herein.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces 'Core Component' as an interchangeable term for 'Base Product'. This creates ambiguity because the 'Contradicted Text' mentions "Royalty Payments will be made only on the Base Product Unit" and "revenue received for Base Product Unit". Introducing another term without clear differentiation in the definition creates a strong possibility of confusion on whether the 'Core Component' also falls under the same royalty scheme, thus undermining the financial section as indicated by the Perturbation Explanation, constituting a Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction, as per the definition.

perturbation.original_text: Enhanced Product means a value added assembly or completed Hearing Aid or Assistive Listening Device which includes the Base Product as one of the component devices. The Enhanced Product may include other electronic components, flex circuitry, microphones, a receiver, plastic housings, volume controls, trimmer potentiometers, push button switches, programming connectors and other components.
perturbation.changed_text: Enhanced Product, also known as 'Advanced Offering', means a value added assembly or completed Hearing Aid or Assistive Listening Device which includes the Base Product as one of the component devices. The Enhanced Product may include other electronic components, flex circuitry, microphones, a receiver, plastic housings, volume controls, trimmer potentiometers, push button switches, programming connectors and other components.
perturbation.explanation: The introduction of 'Advanced Offering' as an alternative to 'Enhanced Product' introduces ambiguity in clauses related to sales and royalty calculations. While the definition suggests equivalence, the contract does not consistently use both terms. This raises the possibility of IntriCon arguing that certain sales should not be subject to royalty payments if they are classified as 'Advanced Offerings' but not explicitly 'Enhanced Products.' Furthermore, this affects section 5, relating to reports, because the contract may not specify the volume of Advanced Offerings, which are the exact products subject to royalty payments.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Within thirty (30) days of the end of each payment quarter IntriCon must provide to Dynamic Hearing a statement of the actual number and value of Base Product Units Sold along with the number and value of DSP features/models sold with those Base Product Units by it or any Related Body Corporate. The statement must also include calculations of the per unit royalty in accordance with clause 4.9. Any royalty due for payment by IntriCon to Dynamic Hearing is payable at the same time the statement is provided.
perturbation.justification: The introduction of 'Advanced Offering' as an alternative term for 'Enhanced Product' creates a contradiction with the 'Contradicted Text' which specifies reporting requirements only for 'Base Product Units Sold' and 'DSP features/models'. The 'Perturbation Explanation' clarifies that this ambiguity affects royalty calculations and reporting, as IntriCon could argue that sales classified as 'Advanced Offerings' are not subject to royalty payments if they are not explicitly 'Enhanced Products'. This directly impacts the obligation stated in the contradicted text, creating a strong in-text contradiction as it introduces uncertainty regarding the scope of royalty calculations and reporting. The changed text causes Misaligned Terminology In Text Contradiction, as it introduces ambiguity in clauses related to sales and royalty calculations. While the definition suggests equivalence, the contract does not consistently use both terms, violating the Perturbation Definitions.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information means information in relation to a party, including its business activities that in connection with this Agreement whether the information is in oral, visual or written form or is recorded or embodied in any other medium and includes all such information disclosed to, or accessed by, the other party before this Agreement commences;
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information, also termed 'Proprietary Data,' means information in relation to a party, including its business activities that in connection with this Agreement whether the information is in oral, visual or written form or is recorded or embodied in any other medium and includes all such information disclosed to, or accessed by, the other party before this Agreement commences;
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'Proprietary Data' as an alternative term for 'Confidential Information,' without clarifying the scope and the specific clauses it applies to, creates potential for in-text contradiction. Specifically, the introduction of the alternative term 'Proprietary Data' may allow a party to argue that certain information, while confidential, does not fall under the obligations related to 'Confidential Information' if they can argue it is treated differently as 'Proprietary Data' under their internal policies. The in-text contradiction is more notable in section 7.2, where it uses 'Confidential Information' and potentially leaves out 'Proprietary Data.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement is confidential and each party must not disclose any part of this Agreement to any person without the prior written consent of the other party.
perturbation.justification: The introduction of 'Proprietary Data' as an alternative to 'Confidential Information' creates a potential ambiguity. The contradicted text states "This Agreement is confidential and each party must not disclose any part of this Agreement to any person without the prior written consent of the other party." If 'Confidential Information' is now potentially distinct from 'Proprietary Data,' a party could argue that the non-disclosure obligation applies only to 'Confidential Information' and not to 'Proprietary Data,' leading to a strong in-text contradiction regarding the scope of confidentiality.
